current_fact.fact_text: On April 27, 1539, the city of Bogotá was officially founded high on the Andean plateau of New Granada, today’s Colombia. Built atop older indigenous settlements, it became a crossroads where European, Indigenous, and African cultures slowly braided into something new.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

current_fact.match_reason: A story of Latin American cultural fusion fits a founding city that later helped shape the region’s urban soundscapes.

queue.fact_text: By 1667, the poet John Milton was blind, politically out of favor, and nearly destitute when he sold the copyright for his epic Paradise Lost for just £10. The poem he parted with so cheaply would later be hailed as one of the greatest works in English literature.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: Milton’s overlooked, underpaid masterpiece reflects the wabi‑sabi idea that value isn’t always visible or polished in its own time.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 1805, a small force of U.S. Marines and local allies stormed the North African port city of Derna during the First Barbary War. The daring overland march across the desert to take a coastal fortress is immortalized in the U.S. Marines’ Hymn as the “shores of Tripoli.”

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A nautical, journey‑themed episode mirrors the seaborne campaign and coastal assault on Derna’s distant shores.

queue.fact_text: In 1927, Chile created the Carabineros de Chile, a national police force and gendarmerie, by merging several regional services into a single green‑uniformed corps. Their mission was as much about nation‑building and symbolism as law enforcement—literally putting a new face on the state.

episode.title: Morning Drops #06 - Friend's Home
episode.description: Yasmin and Gaia are reunited under the same roof to spend Easter Monday morning together, exploring that heartwarming 'just like home' feeling that comes from being surrounded by beloved friends.

queue.match_reason: A show about feeling protected and at home parallels the founding of a national force meant to create order and a sense of shared shelter.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 1941, German troops entered Athens, and the Greek capital fell under Axis occupation. For years, daily life in this ancient city unfolded under black flags, ration cards, censorship, and quiet acts of resistance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A Mediterranean voyage with Greek music offers a gentler counter‑current to the dark years when Athens’ harbors were occupied.

queue.fact_text: In 1953, the U.S. Air Force launched Operation Moolah, broadcasting an offer of up to $100,000 to any pilot who defected to South Korea with a flyable Soviet MiG‑15. It was a bizarre mix of psychological warfare and aviation espionage, dangling a fortune for a single stolen jet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode orbit around technology and psychology—how information, signals, and incentives shape what humans dare to do.

queue.fact_text: Expo 67 opened in Montreal on April 27, 1967, as a futuristic world’s fair where visitors could ride monorails, walk through geodesic domes, and glimpse optimistic visions of the 21st century. For six months, an island in the St. Lawrence River became a temporary planet of architecture, art, and experimental design.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

queue.match_reason: Expo 67’s techno‑utopian vibe pairs with an episode celebrating machines that reshaped music, dance, and visions of the future.

queue.fact_text: The Saur Revolution began in Afghanistan on April 27, 1978, when communist forces overthrew and killed President Mohammed Daoud Khan overnight. It was a short, violent pivot that hurled the country into decades of war, foreign intervention, and shifting regimes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A coup born from ideology and rupture resonates with a band and episode centered on political rage and structural upheaval.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 1986, the city of Pripyat and nearby settlements were ordered to evacuate following the Chernobyl nuclear disaster. Residents boarded buses believing they’d return in a few days; their toys, photo albums, and laundry on the line are still there, frozen in radioactive time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A reflective, planetary‑themed episode provides a contemplative backdrop for thinking about technology, fallout, and the fragility of home.

queue.fact_text: In 1987, the U.S. Department of Justice placed Austrian president and former UN Secretary‑General Kurt Waldheim on a watchlist, barring him from entering the United States. Declassified evidence tied him to Nazi units involved in deportations and executions, turning a celebrated diplomat into a symbol of suppressed wartime truth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

queue.match_reason: Waldheim’s case forced societies to re‑examine comforting narratives about respected figures—exactly the kind of rethinking this episode invites.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 1992, the Russian Federation and 12 other former Soviet republics officially joined the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank. Overnight, states that had planned their economies for decades were negotiating loans, reforms, and a turbulent dive into global capitalism.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: A journey through post‑Soviet landscapes and music aligns with the moment former Soviet republics formally hooked into global financial routes.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 1993, most of Zambia’s national football team died in a plane crash off the coast of Gabon while traveling to a World Cup qualifier. The nation later rebuilt a new team that, remarkably, won the Africa Cup of Nations in 2012—dedicating the victory to the players lost over the sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: A reflective, ocean‑tinged episode suits a story of loss at sea, memory, and the slow, rhythmic rebuilding of a national dream.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 2007, Estonian authorities removed the Bronze Soldier, a Soviet World War II memorial in Tallinn, from its prominent city center location. The relocation of a single statue triggered riots, cyberattacks, and a diplomatic crisis—showing how fiercely the past can live inside public monuments.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

queue.match_reason: Both the statue and the episode deal with what we choose to foreground or background—how curated symbols and stories shape identity and memory.

queue.fact_text: Also in April 2007, Israeli archaeologists announced they had located the long‑sought tomb of Herod the Great at Herodium, a man‑made hill south of Jerusalem. The discovery capped a decades‑long search for the burial site of the king who rebuilt the Second Temple yet is remembered as a tyrant in many traditions.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: Arabic melodies and medina atmospheres match the mood of an ancient Judean hill revealing the tomb of a controversial king.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 2011, a historic “Super Outbreak” of tornadoes ripped through the southeastern United States, with 205 tornadoes touching down in a single day. Entire neighborhoods were scoured from their foundations, and radar images captured storms with multiple vortexes spiraling like living creatures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

queue.match_reason: An episode built around storms, rain, and resilience pairs directly with the most intense tornado outbreak in U.S. history.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 2018, North Korean leader Kim Jong-un stepped over a concrete border line to meet South Korean President Moon Jae-in in the village of Panmunjom. Their Panmunjom Declaration pledged to work toward formally ending the Korean War and denuclearizing the peninsula, offering a rare glimpse of hope on one of Earth’s tensest borders.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

queue.match_reason: The slow work of peace and de‑escalation parallels the episode’s focus on gradual progress, effort, and the long learning curve of change.

queue.fact_text: On April 27, 2007, Estonia also became the target of one of the world’s first large‑scale politically motivated cyberattacks, following the Bronze Soldier dispute. Websites of banks, media, and government agencies were flooded with traffic, foreshadowing how future conflicts would spill into the digital realm.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A cyber onslaught against a whole country matches an episode reflecting on our deep, fragile reliance on digital networks and devices.

queue.fact_text: The Bronze Soldier crisis in Estonia highlighted how a single Soviet‑era war memorial could mean liberation to one community and occupation to another. Moving the statue from downtown Tallinn to a military cemetery turned a piece of metal into a flashpoint for identity, memory, and belonging.

episode.title: Morning Drops #29 - Berber France Meets Arabic Italy
episode.description: Yasmin takes us back to her university years, where she met Abdel - an Algerian-French friend with a big heart and endless optimism. Their friendship grew around music, identity, and playful arguments about whether a song was originally Italian or French. Music that exists in both versions, alongside stories of finding a home, boxing training, and car rides filled with music.

queue.match_reason: An episode about contested identities and feeling at home in more than one culture mirrors how the Bronze Soldier carries different meanings for different groups.

queue.fact_text: The city of Pripyat, evacuated on April 27, 1986, was once a model Soviet “atomgrad” built for nuclear plant workers and their families—complete with an amusement park that never officially opened. Its rusting Ferris wheel has become an eerie symbol of a future that never arrived.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A reflective episode about interrupted movement and slow recovery fits an abandoned city frozen between past plans and an unwritten future.

queue.fact_text: Ferdinand Magellan’s expedition left Spain with five ships, but only one vessel, the Victoria, returned in 1522 after circling the globe. Of the roughly 270 men who set out, just 18 completed the journey—yet their worn hull and star charts proved that Earth could be looped by sail.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A sonic voyage titled “Destination” Sedna mirrors the epic circumnavigation: long, risky, and ultimately expanding the map of what’s possible.

published.fact_text: On April 27, 711, Moorish troops led by the Berber general Tariq ibn Ziyad landed at Gibraltar, a rock that still bears his name in Arabic—Jabal Ṭāriq, “Tariq’s Mountain”. This nighttime landing marked the beginning of the Islamic conquest of the Iberian Peninsula and centuries of cultural fusion in Al-Andalus.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Desert blues and Tuareg stories echo North African histories of movement and conquest, mirroring Tariq’s crossing from the Maghreb into Iberia.

published.fact_text: In 395, the Eastern Roman emperor Arcadius married Aelia Eudoxia, daughter of the Frankish general Flavius Bauto. Though often overlooked, Eudoxia became one of the most powerful empresses of Late Antiquity, shaping imperial politics from behind layers of ceremony and marble.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: An episode celebrating powerful women pairs with the story of Eudoxia, a woman quietly bending an empire’s storyline.

published.fact_text: On April 27, 1521, Ferdinand Magellan was killed at the Battle of Mactan in the Philippines while intervening in a local conflict he barely understood. His death meant he never completed the first circumnavigation of Earth—yet his voyage still redrew humanity’s mental map of the planet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A meditative take on travel and perspective resonates with Magellan’s world‑spanning voyage and its unintended consequences.

published.fact_text: In 1522, the Battle of Bicocca near Milan showed that dense lines of infantry with early firearms could shred even elite Swiss pikemen in minutes. This brief, brutal clash signaled the end of Swiss mercenary dominance and the dawn of gunpowder‑driven warfare in Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden, decisive impact of firearms on the battlefield mirrors the jolt of electrified guitars reshaping the soundscape.

published.fact_text: On April 27, 1539, the city of Bogotá was officially founded high on the Andean plateau of New Granada, today’s Colombia. Built atop older indigenous settlements, it became a crossroads where European, Indigenous, and African cultures slowly braided into something new.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: A story of Latin American cultural fusion fits a founding city that later helped shape the region’s urban soundscapes.
